title: Stochastic Cluster Expansion for Excited State Energies
authors: Annabelle Canestraight, Russell Miller, Libor Veis, Vojtech Vlcek
abstract: Excited-state electronic structure in strongly correlated systems remains challenging due to the exponential scaling of the many-body Hilbert space and the difficulty of constructing systematically controlled active spaces. Building on the stochastic cluster expansion (SCE) framework previously developed for ground-state correlation energies, we extend the formalism to excitation gaps by expressing energy differences directly as a hierarchy of orbital-space cluster contributions. In this formulation, excitation energies are reconstructed from reduced-rank calculations involving a minimal frontier chemical subspace (FCS), treated exactly, together with stochastic sampling of the remaining orbital environment. This approach eliminates the need for large or chemically preselected active spaces. We demonstrate the method on charge-transfer complexes and polyacenes, where accurate singlet-triplet gaps are obtained that agree with full-system results. The method converges with low-order cluster terms and provides a systematically improvable framework for excited states in correlated systems.
summary: This paper introduces a stochastic cluster expansion method to calculate excited-state energy gaps in large, strongly correlated molecular systems. By treating a small active space exactly and sampling the rest of the orbital environment stochastically, the method achieves high accuracy with a massive reduction in computational complexity. It demonstrates success on benchmark systems like polyacenes and charge-transfer complexes.
main_problem: Calculating excited-state electronic structures in strongly correlated systems is difficult due to the exponential scaling of the many-body Hilbert space and the challenge of constructing controlled active spaces.
main_result: The Stochastic Cluster Expansion (SCE) framework successfully extends to excitation gaps, providing accurate singlet-tricept gaps for polyacenes and charge-transfer complexes with a significantly reduced Hamiltonian dimension.
method: An extension of the stochastic cluster expansion (SCE) that expresses energy differences as a hierarchy of orbital-space cluster contributions, using a minimal frontier chemical subspace (FCS) treated exactly and a stochastic sampling of the remaining orbital environment.
model_or_system: Strongly correlated electronic systems, specifically charge-transfer complexes (e.g., Naphthalene-TCNE) and polyacenes (up to heptacene), modeled using many-body Hamiltonians in an orbital-based framework.
key_observables: Excitation gaps, specifically singlet-triplet (S0-T1) energy gaps.
important_parameters: Number of orbitals in the Frontier Chemical Subspace (FCS), number of stochastic samples (N_zeta), and the cluster expansion order.
main_assumptions: Assumes correlation is sufficiently localized for truncated expansion; assumes the excitation is predominantly captured within the defined FCS; assumes the energy gap is evaluated between states sharing nearly the same eigenvectors across stochastic samplings.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows gap renormalization vs FCS size for Naphthalene-TCNE; Fig 2 illustrates gap renormalization for Benzene-TCNE, error convergence vs number of samples, and error per electron; Fig 3 compares singlet-triplet gaps across polyacene lengths and compares Hilbert space dimensions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of excited-state scaling, presents the SCE formalism for energy differences, describes the partitioning into FCS and stochastic rest-space, demonstrates the method on charge-transfer complexes and polyacenes, and discusses error convergence and limitations.
why_interesting: The method provides a scalable way to handle large-scale many-body problems by using stochastic sampling to reduce the effective dimension of the Hilbert space, which is highly relevant for simulating large-scale quantum dynamics and electronic structure.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The S0 −T1 gap computed for a range of FCS sizes in a Naphthalene–TCNE charge-transfer complex. Error bars denote the standard error of the mean for 25 samples. (b) The Naphthalene–TCNE complex, with the Naphthalene HOMO and TCNE LUMO shown.

FIG. 2. (a) Gap renormalization for Benzene–TCNE and Naphthalene–TCNE, compared with full DMRG. Error bars denote the standard error of the mean for 25 samples. (b) Standard error of the mean as a function of the number of stochastic samples. (c) Standard error of the mean per electron.

FIG. 3. (a) Singlet–triplet gap as a function of n-acene length. Results are shown for the FCS-only calculation (HOMO and π∗), the SCE method, and DMRG+PDFT calculations[12]. (b) Dimension of the full Hilbert space for the complete π subspace compared to the largest active space used within the SCE method.
